title: Staff+ Blind Spots — Saying No, Owning Failure & Pushing Back
description: The hardest Staff+ skills have nothing to do with technical ability. Killing your own project, owning a production incident you caused, pushing back on a VP's bad idea, and handling an underperforming peer. This is the content no one teaches but every Staff+ engineer must master. SAP engineers come from a culture where failure is hidden and saying no is uncooperative — FAANG expects the exact opposite.

sections.title: Killing Your Own Project — The Hardest Decision

description.value:
Killing a project you have invested months of work in is the single hardest decision a Staff+ engineer faces. The sunk cost is real — you have time, emotional energy, and reputation invested. But the Staff+ skill is recognizing when continuing is more expensive than stopping, and having the courage to recommend termination.

When to kill a project: (1) The core assumption has been invalidated — you built a feature assuming customers wanted it, and early data shows they don't. (2) The technical approach has fundamental flaws — the architecture cannot scale, the performance cannot meet requirements, the security model is unsound. (3) The business priority has shifted — a higher-priority initiative has emerged that requires your team's capacity. (4) The cost of continuing exceeds the expected value — you are spending $2M/year on a project that will generate $500K in value.

How to kill a project without destroying your reputation: (1) Lead with data, not emotion. 'We have invested 6 months and $1.2M. The early adoption data shows only 5% of target users have tried the feature. At this rate, we will not hit the adoption target within the budget.' (2) Propose the kill, don't wait for someone else. The Staff+ engineer who proactively recommends killing their own project is seen as objective and data-driven, not as a quitter. (3) Have a transition plan. 'I recommend we: (a) stop active development, (b) communicate the decision to stakeholders by Friday, (c) document the learnings in a postmortem, and (d) reassign the team to the fraud detection initiative, which has a higher expected ROI.' (4) Own the decision. 'I believe this is the right call for the company. I led this project, and I am recommending we kill it based on the data.'

The 'kill criteria' pattern: Before starting any significant project, define what success looks like AND what failure looks like. 'We will launch the feature. If adoption is below 10% after 3 months, we will kill it.' This makes the killing decision data-driven rather than emotional. It also protects your reputation because the criteria were established upfront, before you were invested.

SAP Bridge: In SAP consulting, projects are rarely killed. They are signed off as 'lessons learned' in a deck no one reads. The contract must be fulfilled. At FAANG, killing projects is seen as a sign of strategic maturity. The SAP engineer must learn that stopping a failing project is more respected than completing it.

decisionTree.firstQuestion: Has a core assumption of the project been invalidated?

branches.condition: Yes — a core assumption (customer need, technical feasibility, market demand) has been proven wrong
branches.path: Kill the project immediately. Document the invalidated assumption and the data that disproved it. This is a successful learning outcome, not a failure. The team learned something valuable at the cost of the project investment.

branches.condition: No — core assumptions hold, but execution is behind schedule or over budget
branches.path: Do not kill yet. Reset scope and timeline. 'We are behind but the core thesis is valid. Let me propose a revised plan with a 6-week checkpoint. If we don't hit the checkpoint metrics, we kill at that point.'

branches.condition: Partially — some assumptions hold, but the business priority has shifted
branches.path: Evaluate whether the project's value still exceeds its cost under the new priorities. If the business priority change is significant (e.g., company pivoted to AI and your project is on legacy infrastructure), kill and redirect resources. If the change is minor, continue with adjusted scope.

branches.condition: No — but I am personally tired of this project and want to move on
branches.path: This is not a valid reason to kill a project. Find a way to delegate, compartmentalize, or take a break. Killing a valuable project because of personal fatigue is a reputation-damaging move. The Staff+ skill is managing your own energy without dropping commitments.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: When in doubt, set a 2-4 week checkpoint with specific metrics. 'If we don't hit X by [date], we kill.' This removes the emotional weight from the decision and replaces it with data. The checkpoint approach is especially useful for SAP engineers who struggle with the finality of killing a project.

sections.title: Owning Failure — The 'I Caused the Outage' Moment

description.value:
Every Staff+ engineer will eventually cause a production incident. A bad deployment, a missing edge case, a performance regression. How you handle that moment defines your reputation far more than the incident itself. At FAANG, the culture is blameless postmortems — but that requires you to be honest about your role.

The incident ownership pattern: (1) Immediately declare the incident. Don't try to fix it quietly. The longer you wait, the worse the impact. 'I deployed a change to the payment service at 2:00 PM. I am seeing a 5x increase in error rates. I am rolling back now.' (2) Take responsibility in the postmortem. 'The root cause was a missing null check in my code change. I did not test the edge case of empty payment amounts. The mitigation was rollback within 4 minutes.' (3) Propose systemic fixes. 'To prevent this from happening again, I recommend: (a) adding a null-check linter rule, (b) including empty payment amount in the test suite, and (c) implementing canary deployment for all payment service changes.' (4) Stay objective. Don't be defensive. Don't blame external factors. The team will respect you more for owning it than for deflecting it.

Why owning failure builds trust: (1) It shows you prioritize the team's learning over your ego. (2) It demonstrates that you understand systems thinking — the fix is not 'be more careful' but 'improve the system so this can't happen again.' (3) It makes you a safer person to work with. If you own your mistakes, others feel safe owning theirs. This is the foundation of a high-trust engineering culture. (4) It's memorable. Engineers who own their failures are trusted with more responsibility because they have demonstrated they can handle accountability.

SAP Bridge: In SAP, incidents are handled through a strict ticket-and-escalation process. Root cause analysis is often about finding who to blame so the contract SLA can be enforced. At FAANG, root cause analysis is about finding the systemic gap that allowed the incident — and the person who caused it is often the person who leads the postmortem. SAP engineers must shift from 'who is responsible' to 'what allowed this to happen.'

sections.codeBlock:
# Postmortem Ownership Template

## Summary
- **Incident:** Payment service elevated error rates
- **Date:** 2026-06-25
- **Duration:** 4 minutes (2:00 PM - 2:04 PM)
- **Impact:** 5x error rate increase, 0 revenue loss (caught by automated monitoring before customer impact)

## Root Cause
My code change (commit abc123) introduced a null pointer exception when processing payment amounts. The change was tested with standard payment amounts but not with the empty-amount edge case (which occurs when the payment retry mechanism sends a request without an amount).

## Why I Made This Mistake
1. I was not aware of the empty-amount edge case triggered by the retry mechanism
2. The test suite did not include this edge case
3. The change was deployed directly to production without a canary (expedited fix for a P1 bug)

## Systemic Fixes
1. Add null-check linter rule to CI pipeline [owner: me, deadline: this sprint]
2. Add empty payment amount to unit and integration test suite [owner: me, deadline: this sprint]
3. Implement canary deployment for payment service [owner: platform team, deadline: next quarter]

## What I Learned
Production changes need canary deployment even for 'simple' fixes. I will not skip the canary process again, even for expedited fixes.

sections.title: Pushing Back on Executives — The 'Yes, And' Technique

description.value:
One of the most stressful situations for a Staff+ engineer: a VP proposes a technically unsound approach. Do you agree to avoid conflict, or do you push back and risk your relationship with leadership? The Staff+ answer is: push back — but do it in a way that strengthens your relationship rather than damages it.

The 'Yes, And' technique for executive pushback: (1) Validate their goal. 'Yes, I agree that reducing payment processing latency is critical for our revenue growth.' (2) Acknowledge their reasoning. 'And I understand why the in-memory caching approach seems like the right solution — it's simple and we can ship it quickly.' (3) Introduce the constraint they may not have considered. 'The challenge is that our payment system has strong consistency requirements because of regulatory compliance. In-memory caching introduces the risk of stale data being used for reconciliation.' (4) Propose an alternative that achieves the same goal. 'Instead of in-memory caching, I recommend we: (a) optimize the database queries that are the actual bottleneck (estimated 30% improvement), and (b) implement read replicas for the payment data (estimated 50% improvement). This achieves the latency goal without the consistency risk.'

When not to push back: (1) If the executive's proposal is workable, even if it's not optimal. Sometimes 'good enough' is the right call for business reasons you don't fully understand. (2) If the executive has more context than you. 'I may be missing context. Is there a specific reason you are recommending this approach?' (3) If the decision is low-impact. Not every hill is worth dying on. Save your political capital for the decisions that matter.

The 'default yes' culture vs 'disagree and commit': Amazon's 'disagree and commit' principle is the gold standard for executive pushback. You are expected to disagree openly — but once a decision is made, you commit fully. 'I disagree with this approach, and here is why. If you decide to proceed, I will commit to making it work and will share my concerns in writing so we can track whether the risks materialize.' This is not insubordination — it is exactly what Amazon expects from its leaders.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "If I push back on a VP, won't they see me as difficult or not a team player? In SAP culture, you don't challenge senior leadership."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "FAANG culture is different. You must push back on bad ideas." (unhelpful, doesn't address the real fear)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "That fear is valid — in many organizational cultures, challenging senior leadership is career-limiting. At FAANG, the dynamic is different. Executives at FAANG are evaluated on outcomes, not authority. A VP who ships a failed project due to bad technical advice is worse off than a VP who was convinced by a Staff+ engineer to change course. The key is how you push back. You are not saying 'you are wrong.' You are saying 'I want to help us succeed. Here is a risk I see that you may not have considered, and here is a better path to achieve your goal.' When framed as helping the executive succeed, pushback is welcomed. If a VP reacts poorly to respectful, data-driven pushback, that is useful information — it tells you the culture is not as healthy as it should be, and you may want to consider whether this is the right team for you."
adversarialResponse.pattern: executive-alignment — frame pushback as helping the executive succeed. Use the 'Yes, And' pattern: validate their goal first, then introduce the constraint they may not have considered, then propose an alternative that achieves their goal better.

description.value:
One of the most delicate situations in a Staff+ engineer's career: a peer at the same level is underperforming. They miss deadlines, produce low-quality code, or avoid difficult work. You are not their manager, but their performance affects your team's output and your own ability to deliver. How do you handle it?

First, determine the root cause: (1) Skill gap — they genuinely don't know how to do the work. They need training, mentoring, or clearer expectations. (2) Motivation gap — they are capable but not engaged. They may be burnt out, dealing with personal issues, or checked out. (3) Fit gap — they are skilled and motivated but in the wrong role. Their strengths don't match the team's needs. (4) Awareness gap — they don't know they are underperforming. Their previous environment had different standards and no one has given them honest feedback.

How to approach it: (1) Start with empathy. 'I've noticed you seem frustrated with the data pipeline project. How is it going? Is there anything I can help with?' This opens the door without accusation. (2) Be specific about the behavior, not the person. 'I noticed the last three pull requests had significant feedback from the reviewers. Is there something about the codebase that's unclear?' (3) Offer help, not judgment. 'I had trouble with this module too when I first joined. Would it help if we pair-programmed on the next feature?' (4) If the behavior continues, escalate to your manager. 'I'm concerned about X's performance on the payment project. I've tried offering help, but the output quality hasn't improved. I wanted to flag this because it's affecting the team's delivery timeline.' (5) Maintain professionalism throughout. Do not gossip about your peer to other team members. Do not undermine their work publicly. Handle it privately, respectfully, and systematically.

SAP Bridge: In SAP consulting, underperformance is handled by the project manager — you tell the PM and they reassign the person. At FAANG, the cultural expectation is that you first try to help your peer directly. Escalating to your manager without first attempting to help is seen as skipping a step. The SAP engineer should try the direct approach first, but not wait too long — if the peer's performance is affecting the team's delivery, escalate in parallel with your help attempts.

Bad news does not get better with time. A problem that is surfaced early can be solved. A problem that is surfaced late is a crisis. The 'escalate early' pattern is one of the most valuable habits a Staff+ engineer can develop.

What to escalate early: (1) Timeline risks — 'I am 80% confident we will miss the Q3 deadline unless we get the data pipeline team's commitment by Friday.' (2) Technical risks — 'The current architecture may not scale beyond 100K QPS. We need to validate this before we launch.' (3) Resource gaps — 'We have 3 engineers and 5 critical projects. We need to prioritize or hire.' (4) People issues — 'I'm concerned about the conflict between the design and implementation teams. It's blocking progress on the integration.'

How to escalate: (1) State the problem clearly. 'The payment migration timeline is at risk.' (2) State the impact if not addressed. 'If we don't resolve the API dependency by next week, the migration will slip by 3 weeks, delaying the revenue impact by one quarter.' (3) Propose a solution. 'I need you to escalate to the accounting team's VP to prioritize the API review.' (4) State the deadline. 'I need a decision by Thursday to keep the original timeline.' (5) Offer alternatives. 'If the accounting API can't be prioritized, we can defer the payment migration to Q4 and focus on frontend optimization instead.'

The 'escalate with context' rule: Never escalate a problem without context. Your manager needs to understand: (a) what the problem is, (b) what caused it, (c) what the impact is, (d) what you have tried, (e) what you need from them, and (f) what the alternatives are. If you escalate without context, you are just dumping a problem on your manager's lap. If you escalate with context and a proposed solution, you are being a leader.

sections.title: Protecting Your Team from Bad Decisions from Above

description.value:
A classic Staff+ scenario: leadership decides to take a technical direction that you know is wrong. Maybe it's adopting a technology that doesn't fit the problem. Maybe it's a timeline that is impossible. Maybe it's an architectural approach that will create massive technical debt. Your job is to protect your team from the consequences while maintaining a good relationship with leadership.

The 'umbrella' pattern: (1) Absorb the pressure at your level. When leadership says 'this must ship by Q1,' don't pass the pressure to your team. 'The VP wants this by Q1. I believe the timeline is aggressive. I will negotiate the scope. You focus on building the right thing, and I will handle the timeline conversation.' (2) Create a buffer. 'I will shield the team from the organizational noise. You focus on the technical work. If there are any surprises, I will let you know.' (3) Translate leadership direction into team-level priorities. 'Leadership wants us to focus on reliability this quarter. For our team, that means: (a) reduce P0 incidents by 50%, (b) improve monitoring coverage, (c) implement automated rollback. Here is how we will do this without burning out.'

When to push back vs when to shield: (1) If the decision has a workable path, shield the team and execute. 'The timeline is aggressive but achievable if we reduce scope. I will negotiate scope while you build the core.' (2) If the decision is genuinely harmful (e.g., it will create security risks or massive technical debt), push back at the leadership level — not by telling your team 'leadership is wrong' but by going to leadership with data. 'I understand the desire to ship by Q1. Here is the risk: the current approach would require us to skip security review, which could lead to a P0 incident. I recommend we push the timeline by 6 weeks and include security review.' (3) Never undermine leadership to your team. 'The VP doesn't know what they're doing' is toxic and destroys trust. Instead: 'The VP has a different perspective. I've shared our concerns and here is the outcome. We will proceed with the agreed approach.'

details: Staff+ Insight: The blind spots covered here — killing projects, owning failure, pushing back, handling peer performance, escalating early, protecting the team — are the behaviors that separate Staff+ engineers who thrive from those who struggle. They are not taught in any onboarding program. They are not covered in interview prep. They are learned through experience, usually through painful mistakes. The SAP engineer who masters these blind spots has a significant advantage: they bring enterprise-grade judgment and reliability to a FAANG environment that values those traits but often finds them rare. The key insight: at FAANG, these behaviors are not career-negative — they are career-defining in the most positive way. Owning a mistake builds more trust than hiding it. Killing a failing project demonstrates more leadership than completing it. Pushing back on a VP with data earns more respect than agreeing and delivering a bad outcome.
